title: The Macro Minute:  Will Trump’s “Paradigm C” catalyze a bigger boom than Biden’s “Paradigm A”?

Happy Tuesday out there team42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present a macro minute for Tuesday May
6, 2025. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's leadoff
morning note. If you would like to
review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's key macro question is, will
President Trump's paradigm Catalyze a
bigger boom in risk assets than
President Biden's paradigm A? Our
answers are to recap our regime
segmentation framework. Paradigm A is a
bloated K-shaped US economy propped up
by excessive government spending and
over easy monetary policy that
disproportionately flatters income and
capital formation of households and
businesses on the top part of the K at
the expense of crowding out households
and businesses on the bottom part of the
K. Paradigm A is Biden's economy.
Paradigm B is a buoyant E-shaped US
economy that shifts the burden of
generating marginal economic outputs
back to the private sector in a manner
that reduces the disproportional flow of
funds from the public sector to the
private sector and the associated
crowding out. Paradigm B is where the
Trump administration in initially
intended to guide the economy to, but
their miscalculated and quite frankly
arrogant tactics broke the bond market,
which in turn robbed them of their
willingness to tolerate the pain
required to facilitate the transition to
paradigm B. And finally, paradigm C
features all the fiscal and monetary
large of paradigm A, plus more tax cuts
that largely benefit the rich and
corporations, as well as deregulation
that, among other things, will make it
easier to replace American workers with
AI and automation, per our
interpretation of Commerce Secretary
Howard Lutnik's commentary throughout
March and April. Without meaningful
deficit reduction, the crowding out of
paradigm A will persist. Paradigm C is
likely where the Trump administration is
guiding the economy to now after Wall
Street reminded them whose boss on April
9th and April 11th. Main Street families
will likely benefit from even more
trickle down economic exceptionalism
than they did under Paradigm A during
the Biden administration, which is a
positive to be celebrated for sure. But
make no mistake about it, the Trump
administration is no longer on track to
meaningfully narrow the toxic imbalance
indicated by the chart on slide nine in
today's leadoff warning note, uh, which
is the chart of the day we featured uh,
in our, uh, uh, morning note on Twitter,
etc. Uh, as such, the median investor
will likely celebrate the Trump 2.0
economy more than the median US
household. To this point, it is worth
highlighting that the top 20% of US
households by income own roughly 90% of
financial assets and account for roughly
40% of all consumption. The K-shaped
nature of the US economy will not only
survive the par the transition to
paradigm C, it is likely to thrive in
it. This should result in a boom in
asset markets much like par president
President Biden's paradigm A before it.
Whether the boom is larger remains to be
seen, but investors must remain
dispassionate, politically unbiased, and
open-minded to this increasingly
probable outcome. All told, investors
can trust that our kiss and doctor mo
signals will continue to do a worldclass
job of helping their portfolio
successfully capture the bulk of the
uptrends during the boom phases while
booking gains ahead of any substantial
hiccups along the way. And there will be
substantial hiccups to risk manage. FYI,
2025 featured the fourth stock market
crash since 2018 and eighth since
1998. Waiting around for your portfolio
to recover from volatility drag is not a
gamble anyone in or near retirement can
afford to make. One day, risk assets
won't recover as fast as investors my
age have been conditioned to expect. So
you better get with some systematic risk
management if you're going to be
managing a meaningfully sized portfolio.
Uh as we deepen into this fork turning
uh poly crisis uh which will in our
opinion just transitioning to our 42
micro dashboard is always wrapped up the
question for our community. I think this
is a perfect question to sort of help us
understand how the forth turning poly
crisis is likely to deepen from here. Uh
so this one's titled F-shaped economy.
Uh says uh I love the visual of the EK
and E-shaped economies. Would F be an
accurate representation name for
paradigm C? Top stays on top, which is
better uh for the middle uh uh middle
and main street investors and K or I
would say households really uh because
we know they don't really own assets
unfortunately outside of their homes. Uh
but would it be better for middle and
main street households than K and no
support uh um for the bottom of the K uh
like E. So paradigm A is K. this uh the
client is suggesting paradigm B is an
E-shaped economy and paradigm C is uh
they're asking the question would it be
an F-shaped economy? In my opinion, I
don't think so. What I think paradigm C
represents is a even bigger version of
the K. So the top part of the K gets
bigger and the bottom part of the K gets
bigger. So instead so so so instead of
thinking like like right now paradigm uh
paradigm A is essentially an upside down
lowercase K.
Now make that upside down lower click
and drag the corner of that lowercase K
upside down lowercase K and make it
bigger like if you click and drag the
corner of a window on your computer and
made it bigger. That's exactly what
we're talking about in terms of uh in
terms of paradigm C. You know, I think
what's missing from you know, the uh
discussion right now is the lack of
fiscal retrenchment we're going to see,
right? If you think about how fiscal
retrenchment uh, you know, sort of, you
know, kind of a you know, perpetuates
this this this this um essentially a
copy and pasting of the K-shaped
economy. Uh obviously you're going to be
crowding out households and businesses
on the low end of the spectrum when
you're talking about, you know, interest
rates that are much higher than they
otherwise would be because we have this
sustained non-war non-recession budget
deficit of 7% uh in terms of uh in terms
of our uh federal uh budget deficit. Uh
it's our view that you know based on our
analysis uh I think we've done as much
detailed analysis on this as anyone I've
seen out there uh and have been leading
the charge on this this this stuff for
years in terms of understanding all the
puts and takes of the US uh federal
budget dynamics. you know, even if Doge
is exactly what uh they were promising
Doge to be back in late March uh when
Elon and the other heads of the Doge had
that u interview on Fox business, Fox
News or Fox Business, uh they were very,
you know, uh they shared a lot of
information if you if you missed that.
uh even if it was what they essentially
claiming it will be which is a trillion
dollars out of annualized federal
expenditures which at the time
represented 15% of annual federal
expenditures if you float that all the
way down through the P&L and uh over uh
in terms of uh in terms of what that
would ultimately do to the budget
deficit. If you just float that all the
way down to the P&L from the all-time
high in federal expenditures which was
in February uh then you're talking about
a budget deficit that's somewhere around
the order of $2.6 6 trillion which is
roughly about 6% of GDP 5 to 6% of GDP.
So uh if Doge is exactly what it they
they claim it will do and which is an
very aggressive and it has no economic
impact on the economy in a way that
reduces revenue or slows the revenue
growth. You're talking about Doge taking
the federal budget deficit from 7% to
6%. Let's let's use those round numbers.
uh that's obviously not enough to you
know to to to to get a significant
reduction in interest rates that
ultimately causes the an unwind of the
crowding out of the bottom part of the K
from a capital markets perspective. Um
another way in which the fiscal large
ass has been very beneficial for the
those in the top part of the K is
through direct transfers. I mean, I just
found out yesterday that the government
is still paying $70 billion
perom to corporations, to people like
me, wealthy business owners like myself
to keep people employed during CO. Last
year, they paid $70 billion to wealthy
business owners to keep people employed
during CO. And so, this is the kind of
fiscal large that has been very um
regressive uh in a I mean, like if you
go back to co, you know, if you're poor,
you got a $600 extension in your uh
unemployment insurance and and and
instead of it lasting x amount of weeks,
it lasted, you know, maybe, you know, 52
weeks instead of 26 or whatever. I don't
know what the total number of weeks is,
so don't shoot me on that. But what the
rich people got was
PPP. People took their million plus
dollar PPP checks and bought priv
private jets, Lamborghinis. They went on
trips to Monaco. They went on trips.
They've been traveling for the rest of
the world. I mean, you go down to Miami
or anywhere near Palm Beach where all
the ppb money wound up and everybody's
got a new boat. You can't go out to take
your wife to dinner without spending
$1,000 down there. And so, you wonder
why there's so much popular discontent
with how the government is working and
why everyone is so angry at the
government in terms of yeah, Doge, yeah,
waste, fraud, and abuse. If you're
handing out millions of dollars to rich
people to keep people on the dole, which
is doesn't necessarily have to flow down
to their employees, it can just flow
straight down into their their bottom
line as LLC owners. This is what's been
happening for years. And I'm not I'm
just using this as a particular example,
but uh don't forget we got a trill
almost a trillion dollar defense budget
that'll be over a trillion dollars by
the end of this year. Um that money's
not going to poor people. That money is
going to companies like Palanteer and
the rich people who that own the equity
in the companies like Palunteer, right?
I'm just using these as very live
examples. I mean, think about spa Elon
owning SpaceX and selling services to
the government. You know, this money
doesn't go to poor people when the
government spends money. The government
spends money on poor people in via
welfare,
Medicaid, and a few other things. Maybe
the Department of Education, but by and
large, that's what is that probably I
don't know somewhere around 25% of the
the government that when you when you
kind of add it all up. The other 75% is
just going out the door and winding up
as profits as revenue and profits to
wealthy business owners. And so you have
a direct income transfer from the
country to rich people in the form of
government outlays while at the same
time obviously pay most of the taxes. So
they're obviously going to benefit
mostly from the tax uh reductions as
well. So that makes sense. I don't have
any bone to pick with that. If you pay
the most taxes, you should benefit from
the tax cuts. But again, that doesn't
stop the that doesn't that doesn't
doesn't take away the fact that tariffs
are a regressive tax that could be used
to finance tax cuts for wealthy people.
So, um I think the key takeaway from all
this is that the K-shaped nature of the
economy is likely to be persistent in
paradigm C. Uh I think another key
takeaway is that paradigm C is probably
going to be great for the economy and
asset markets in aggregate. However, as
it relates to going back to the fourth
turning poly crisis, which is going to
continue to create political volatility
and as a function of that political
volatility, financial market volatility
of epic proportions, then we're going to
continue to see the income and wealth
inequality that is, you know, consistent
feature of every fork turning that
ultimately catalyzes itself in some
existential threat because people are
angry enough and pissed off enough to go
to a total war or or reorganize the
economy and some other very aggressive
aggressive manner uh that is not
currently foreseen. So um I'm starting
to get a sense of how this all may play
out and don't forget we have our friend
AI coming to take a lot of folks jobs
according to uh the Fiverr CEO and other
CEOs within and around the tech space.
So uh I think the key takeaway is is I
don't think you I think if you have a
multi-year time horizon I think you got
to be very bullish on risk assets as a
function of paradigm C. Uh, I think you
also must have a a deep understanding
and appreciation of where this might
ultimately take us because essentially
it's going to create a even more
distorted topheavy K-shaped
economy over time. And that that that
that topheavy Kshape is essentially
going to wind up and manifest itself in
political volatility. Imagine if you
went back from people got us so
disenchanted with the K getting bigger
in this particular administration that
they swing us all the way back to you
know Camela Harris or by Joe Biden and
you reverse all this deregulation and
tax cuts you know can you imagine the
kind of political volatility which
ultimately winds up in economic
volatility as a function of all this but
again those are multiple trades away I
think you got to trade the trade that's
ahead of you and in my opinion if we're
right on this transition to paradigm C
the big trade that's ahead of us is the
rest of the markets agreeing with
paradigm C as the modal outcome and in
our opinion that's uh that's a much
higher market let's call it a year from
now than we are today. So we'll wrap it
